note: Thanks for the invitation to speak, I greatly appreciate it.
I first want to take a moment to introduce and thank my lab members, without whom the lab wouldn’t function, and my funding sources, which keep the lights on.

My lab has its hands in a lot of things, and like many biomedical engineering labs does a substantial amount of engineering to answer a variety of scientific questions.Given that the focus of today’s VSF is engineering the eye, I’ll go through the engineering side of things, but will point out what kinds of questions we answer with the tools we use.… I’m going to talk about all of these briefly, but with a focus on the novel functional imaging techniques
So the first is high resolution imaging techniques. We actively these

We actively develop a variety of software and hardware enhancements for our in vivo high resolution device called an adaptive optics. We’ve added a bunch…-We regularly take, and work with images obtained from our adaptive optics scanning laser ophthalmoscope, extracting a variety of metrics, from these images-An up and coming modality we’re going to be using for this is an adaptive optics line scanning ophthalmoscope. The images themselves don’t look terribly different than our AOSLO, but

But is more than 10x faster than our AOSLOThat means we can acquire data from a participant 10x faster, corresponding to not only much shorter imaging sessions, but also much more tolerable imaging for folks that have trouble sitting for long exams.
More than that, we are planning to add an OCT arm to the device in collaboration with Chris Langlo startup, making this device a combination AO-LSO-OCT, providing depth information
And in all of theseIf one criticism can be leveled at these images, its that for all of their resolution, they only natively provide information on structural changes to the retina
So a large area of our research is on a novel functional imaging technique called optoretinography

Optoretinography is on its face very simple. While imaging with near-infrared light…… So, you might say, that’s great, the cells twinkle- but I can’t use this in my research unless I know why its twinkling!

And that’s a good question! We’ve put a lot of thought and effort into the “why” of these changes, and what we know so far is that nm scale changes in the photoreceptor outer segments cause our imaging beam to self interfere constructively and destructively over time, which manifests as the twinkling we observe in our videos.In practice, this means we can observe changes in our signals when there is a damaged outer segment

Like in a study done by one of my grad students’ Mina, where she showed that optoretinography not only showed changes in individuals with retinitis pigmentosa, she showed that this method is even more sensitive than microperimetry.
But the best part about these signals is that they’re readily obtainable across multiple different devices and species
and you don’t even need adaptive optics, just the ability to image and stimulate the cells. This is an image from a wide field SLO, where I did the same analysis over the entire field.
We’re also working on developing biomarkers using videos of the vasculature

So if you take a retinal imaging device and focus up on the vasculature, you get a lovely picture of the blood vessels; We’ve been using these images in combination with flicker stimuli to evaluate changes in vessel diameter in response to a stimulus. We also use another technique where we stop our vertical scanner halfway down- by doing this, we trade spatial resolution for temporal resolution, and can determine the flow of a vessel by analyzing the angle of the streaks you see in this video here.

Its my understanding that we’re going to keep doing these research updates, but as this is my first I wanted to provide a high level overview of what my lab does- at present, we have our hands in a number of things
